---
title: "Notebooks vs scripts"
module: "M3 — Data & Tooling"
chapter: 11
difficulty: "beginner"
prereqs:
  - "M1 Ch14 — Notebooks, the IDE & Colab"
learning_objectives:
  - "Explain how a notebook (cells, run in any order) differs from a script (a .py file, run top-to-bottom)"
  - "Recognise the hidden-state trap of running notebook cells out of order"
  - "Choose the right tool: a notebook to explore, a script to automate and reproduce"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 14
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
review_cards:
  - id: notebook-vs-script
    q: "What is the core difference between a notebook and a script?"
    a: "A notebook is a sequence of cells you can run in any order, with results shown inline — great for exploring. A script is a .py file that runs top-to-bottom every time — great for automating and reproducing."
  - id: hidden-state
    q: "What is the hidden-state trap in notebooks?"
    a: "Because you can run cells out of order, a notebook can show a result that no clean top-to-bottom run would reproduce. The fix is to 'Restart & Run All' before trusting it."
  - id: which-tool
    q: "When do you reach for a notebook, and when for a script?"
    a: "A notebook to explore data and see plots inline; a script when the work must run the same way every time — a pipeline, a scheduled job, or shared production code."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=beginner >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

You've been writing code in cells on this page — a **notebook**. But the data jobs
that run every night, retrain a model, or ship in a product are almost always
plain **scripts**: `.py` files run from a terminal. Same Python, two very different
working styles — and knowing when to use each is part of being a data person, not
only a learner.

A notebook lets you run little pieces in any order and see results (and plots)
right next to the code — perfect for *exploring*. A script runs from the first line
to the last, every single time — perfect for *reproducing* and *automating*. This
chapter is the *what and why* of the two, and the one trap that catches everybody.

> You'll need from before: that you've met notebooks, an editor, and Colab
> (M1 Ch14). This page itself is a notebook-style environment.

---

## A notebook: cells, run in any order

A notebook is a list of **cells**. You run one, see its output, tweak it, run it
again — all while the variables you've defined stay alive in memory. That instant
feedback is why notebooks rule **exploratory data analysis**: load a CSV, peek at
it, plot it, adjust, repeat.

The cell below behaves like a notebook cell — it runs, shares its state with the
page, and shows output inline. Press **Run**:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "A cell runs its lines top-to-bottom and shows output inline"
steps = ["load data", "clean it", "plot it"]
for i, step in enumerate(steps, start=1):
    print(f"{i}. {step}")
```

Inside one cell, the lines still run **top-to-bottom**. The freedom — and the
danger — is at the level of *cells*: you can run them in any order you like.

## A script: a .py file, run top-to-bottom

A **script** is a text file ending in `.py`. You run it from a terminal with
`python yourfile.py`, and it executes from the first line to the last, with no
cells and no inline output beyond what you `print`. Run it again tomorrow and it
does exactly the same thing — that predictability is why pipelines and production
code are scripts.

> 💡 **On your own computer** (this page runs in the browser, so the commands below
> are for your real machine, not here). This is a real transcript — a tiny script
> and what `python` actually printed when it ran:
>
> ```bash
> $ cat greet.py
> name = "Larnix"
> total = 2 + 2
> print(f"{name}: 2 + 2 = {total}")
>
> $ python greet.py
> Larnix: 2 + 2 = 4
> ```

No cells, no "run this bit first" — the file ran top-to-bottom and printed one
line. Run it a hundred times and you get that same line a hundred times.

## The trap: hidden state

Because a notebook lets you run cells out of order, it can show a result that a
clean run would never produce. Define a variable in cell 5, use it in cell 2, then
delete cell 5 — cell 2 still "works" because the value is lingering in memory, until
you restart and everything breaks.

> ⚠️ Watch out: before you trust a notebook's results — or share them — use
> **"Restart & Run All"**. If it doesn't run top-to-bottom from a clean start, the
> result isn't real. This is the single most common notebook bug.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 🧱 For Java developers

A script is the familiar model: a `main` you run start-to-finish, like
`java Main`. A notebook is closer to a long-lived REPL/JShell session where earlier
statements stay in scope — powerful for experimenting, but the session's
accumulated state is exactly what makes "it worked in my session" unreproducible.
"Restart & Run All" is the notebook equivalent of a clean rebuild.
:::

## Which one, when?

- **Notebook** — exploring a new dataset, trying plots, explaining an analysis with
  prose and charts side by side (this course is notebooks for that reason).
- **Script** — a cleaning or training step that must run the same way every time, a
  job on a schedule, code another program imports, anything headed for production.

A common real workflow: *explore* in a notebook, then *move the settled code into a
script* so it runs reliably without you.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. A **notebook** is cells you can run in any order with inline output — best for
   **exploring**.
2. A **script** is a `.py` file that runs **top-to-bottom** every time — best for
   **reproducing and automating**.
3. The notebook trap is **hidden state** from out-of-order runs; **"Restart & Run
   All"** before you trust a result.
4. Explore in a notebook, then move the settled code into a script.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

A file's extension tells you what it is. Complete `is_script` so it returns `True`
for a Python script (a name ending in `.py`). Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_isscript
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_isscript
def is_script(filename):
    """True if filename is a Python script (ends with '.py')."""
    return ____  # TODO: does the name end with ".py"?

run_tests([
    ("a script", is_script("clean.py"), True),
    ("a notebook", is_script("explore.ipynb"), False),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Strings have a method `.endswith(...)` that returns `True` or `False`. Try
`filename.endswith(".py")`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def is_script(filename):
    return filename.endswith(".py")
```

`.endswith(".py")` is `True` for `"clean.py"` and `False` for `"explore.ipynb"` —
the extension is how you tell a script from a notebook on disk.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

When you move notebook cells into a script, you join their lines into one file.
Write `make_script(lines)` — join a list of code lines into a single string, one per
line. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_makescript
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_makescript
def make_script(lines):
    """Join code lines into one script string, one line each."""
    return ____  # TODO: join the lines with a newline character

run_tests([
    ("two lines", make_script(["a = 1", "print(a)"]), "a = 1\nprint(a)"),
    ("one line", make_script(["print('hi')"]), "print('hi')"),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`"\n".join(lines)` glues the items together with a newline between each — exactly
how lines sit in a `.py` file.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def make_script(lines):
    return "\n".join(lines)
```

`"\n".join(lines)` puts a newline *between* items (not after the last), so two cells
become two lines of a script — the first step in turning exploration into a
repeatable file.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). In two or three sentences, describe
a data task you'd do in a **notebook** and a different one you'd put in a **script**,
and say why each fits.

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Names a notebook task that is **exploratory** (peeking at data, plotting, trying
  ideas) and says why inline, out-of-order work helps.
- Names a script task that must be **reproducible or automated** (a nightly clean, a
  shared pipeline) and says why top-to-bottom running matters.
- Mentions the explore-then-move-to-a-script workflow, or the "Restart & Run All"
  habit.

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
answer = """
I'd explore a new sales CSV in a notebook: load it, run df.describe(), draw a few
plots, and adjust as I learn — the inline output makes that fast.
I'd put the nightly cleaning step in a script (clean_sales.py) so it runs the same
way every night with no one watching. I explore first, then move the settled code
into the script, and I 'Restart & Run All' the notebook before trusting any result.
"""
print(answer.strip())
```

The split is exploration vs reliability: notebooks for *figuring it out*, scripts
for *running it the same way every time*.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch11.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You know which tool fits which job — and the hidden-state trap that makes "Restart &
Run All" a reflex. Next we tackle the habit that makes *either* one trustworthy on
someone else's machine: **environments & reproducibility** — pinned versions and
fixed random seeds, so your analysis gives the same answer everywhere.
